1: Canaanite psychological collapse: The report that all Amorite and Canaanite kings 'melted in heart' (vayimmas levavam) upon hearing of the Jordan crossing repeats Rahab's description (Joshua 2:11) — confirming that the psychological impact of the miracle was immediate and universal in Canaan. The ANE understanding of divine portents at river crossings is relevant: a river parting before an approaching army would have been interpreted in Canaanite thought as the most powerful divine favor imaginable — the sea and river deities submitting to the Israelite God. The Canaanite kings were not merely militarily discouraged; they were theologically overwhelmed. 'There was no longer any spirit in them' — their own divine system had no answer to YHWH's Jordan miracle.
2: Circumcision at Gilgal: flint knives: 'Make flint knives and circumcise the sons of Israel again' (Joshua 5:2) — the use of flint (tzur, H6697) rather than metal knives for circumcision is a deliberate archaism. Zipporah circumcised Moses's son with a flint (Exodus 4:25); the priestly tradition preserved flint for circumcision even when metal knives were available. In Egyptian practice, circumcision with flint knives is attested in wall paintings from Saqqara (Old Kingdom). The Egyptian practice may have influenced Israelite ritual, or both may reflect a common archaic tradition. The use of flint preserves the pre-metallurgical sacred character of the rite — some things must be done the old way precisely to maintain their connection to the original covenant.
3: Gibeath-haaraloth: hill of the foreskins: 'Joshua made flint knives and circumcised the sons of Israel at Gibeath-haaraloth' (Joshua 5:3) — 'Hill of the Foreskins' is either a place name derived from this event or a pre-existing local toponym. Place names derived from significant events were common in Israel (Beer-sheba from the well-covenant, Peniel from the face-of-God wrestling). The mass circumcision of an entire generation — all males born in the wilderness who had not been circumcised — was a surgical undertaking of enormous scale. The recovery period (Joshua 5:8 — 'they remained in their places in the camp until they were healed') placed the entire army in a vulnerable state (cf. Dinah incident, Genesis 34:25) — an act of deliberate reliance on YHWH's protection during the healing period.
4: Why the wilderness generation was not circumcised: The explanation in Joshua 5:4-7 is historically precise: all the circumcised males who came out of Egypt had died in the wilderness (the forty-year judgment, Numbers 14:29-35); the males born in the wilderness had never been circumcised. This means the conquest army consisted entirely of uncircumcised men — not one of the fighting men of Israel was in covenant status as they prepared to fight YHWH's holy war. The priority of covenant identity (circumcision) over military readiness (entering battle uncircumcised) reflects the theology of the conquest: the battle belongs to YHWH, and YHWH's warriors must be covenant-marked before they fight as his agents.
5: Born in the wilderness: a liminal generation: The generation born in the wilderness between the Exodus and the Jordan crossing occupied an unusual theological and sociological position. They had not experienced Egypt (they were born free in the wilderness) but had grown up under judgment (their parents' generation was excluded from the land). They had never been circumcised — the primary identity marker of covenant membership. The mass circumcision at Gilgal was thus a generational initiation rite: this generation formally entered the covenant that their parents had already received at Sinai but that they themselves had not been marked with. Gilgal completed the covenant membership of the conquest generation.
6: Forty years in the wilderness: The forty-year wilderness period (Joshua 5:6) was a precise judicial sentence (Numbers 14:33-34: forty days of spying = forty years of wandering, a year per day). The generation that refused to trust YHWH at Kadesh-barnea (Num 13-14) bore the punishment of dying outside the land. The wilderness generation's death is described in stark terms: 'they did not obey the voice of YHWH; YHWH swore that he would not let them see the land which YHWH had sworn to their fathers to give us.' The land flowing with milk and honey (Joshua 5:6) is described not as what Israel received but as what the disobedient generation forfeited — a warning embedded in the conquest's origin story.
7: "Their children, whom he raised up in their place": 'Their children, whom he raised up in their place, Joshua circumcised' (Joshua 5:7) — the conquest generation is explicitly a replacement generation. YHWH raised up new sons to replace the faithless fathers, a pattern of generational replacement under divine sovereignty that recurs in biblical narrative (Abraham's line through Isaac rather than Ishmael, Jacob chosen over Esau). The wilderness generation died without fulfilling the promise; their children did not inherit their failure but received the promise fresh. This generational structure is the theological resolution to the Numbers 13-14 crisis: YHWH's purpose cannot be permanently thwarted by one generation's failure.
8: Healing period: vulnerability and trust: 'They remained in their places in the camp until they were healed' (Joshua 5:8) — the mass circumcision created a period of maximum military vulnerability. An uncircumcised army could not attack; a recently circumcised army could not defend. In the Dinah narrative, the men of Shechem were circumcised and were attacked on the third day 'when they were sore' (Genesis 34:25) — the vulnerability was well-known. Israel's willingness to undergo this vulnerability at the threshold of Canaan, with Jericho's garrison potentially aware of their presence (the Jordan crossing could not have been secret), was an act of faith in YHWH's promise of protection. The conquest begins not with military readiness but with covenant compliance.
9: Gilgal: rolling away Egypt's reproach: 'YHWH said to Joshua, today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you' (Joshua 5:9) — the name Gilgal is given its theological etymology: galal (to roll, H1556). The 'reproach of Egypt' was the shame of being enslaved, uncircumcised (circumcision was an Egyptian practice too, but for the priestly/royal class — common slaves were uncircumcised in Egypt's social hierarchy), and under judgment in the wilderness. At Gilgal, all three dimensions of Egypt's reproach were lifted: slavery was over (forty years past), circumcision was completed, and the wilderness judgment-period had ended. The naming of the place as Gilgal preserved this theological moment: Gilgal literally embodied the rolling-away of shame and the beginning of a new identity as possessors of the land.
10: Passover on the fourteenth of Nisan in Canaan: 'While the people of Israel were encamped at Gilgal, they kept the Passover on the fourteenth day of the month in the evening' (Joshua 5:10) — this was the first Passover celebrated in the land of Canaan. The Passover at Gilgal completes the liturgical arc begun at the first Passover in Egypt (Exod 12): the first Passover marked the beginning of the Exodus; this Passover marks its completion. Passover in the plains of Moab (if it was celebrated there, which is uncertain) would have been the last in the wilderness; the Gilgal Passover is the first in the land. The Passover lamb's blood marked the doors of the faithful in Egypt; circumcision marked the covenant people at Gilgal — both rites mark the boundary between death and life, judgment and salvation.
11: Eating the produce of the land: transition from manna: 'On the day after the Passover, on that very day, they ate of the produce of the land, unleavened cakes and parched grain' (Joshua 5:11) — the produce of Canaan was consumed for the first time on the sixteenth of Nisan, the day after the Passover offering. The unleavened cakes (matzot) eaten at Passover were now made from Canaanite grain rather than wilderness manna — a tangible, edible sign that the land had been entered. The transition from manna to land produce was not gradual but immediate: on the day after Passover, they ate from the land.
12: Manna ceased: ending of wilderness provision: 'The manna ceased the day after they ate of the produce of the land' (Joshua 5:12) — the cessation of manna is dated precisely: the day after they ate Canaanite produce. The manna had sustained Israel for forty years (Exodus 16:35 — 'the people ate manna forty years, until they came to a habitable land'). Its cessation is not described as loss but as fulfillment: the extraordinary provision of the wilderness was appropriate to wilderness conditions; land-based provision is appropriate to settled land. The ending of manna is an act of YHWH's faithfulness — having sustained Israel supernaturally through the desert, he transitions them to natural provision in the land. No more manna was needed because the land itself was YHWH's provision.
13: Man with drawn sword: Christophany context: 'Joshua lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, a man was standing before him with his drawn sword in his hand' (Joshua 5:13) — the figure appears immediately after the Passover, as Joshua faces the first military objective (Jericho). The drawn sword (cherev) identifies this as a military figure, and the question 'are you for us, or for our adversaries?' establishes that Joshua initially processed him as a human soldier of unknown allegiance. The appearance of a divine messenger in human form before a critical military engagement has ANE parallels: Assyrian kings reported divine beings appearing before them before battle to promise victory. In Israel's tradition, such appearances are theophanies (Gen 18, 32) or angel-of-YHWH encounters.
14: "Commander of the army of YHWH": 'I am the commander of the army of YHWH (sar-tzva-YHWH)' — the title designates the leader of the heavenly host, the divine army of angels that fights YHWH's battles. The 'army of YHWH' (tzva YHWH) includes both angelic warriors and the stars (Judges 5:20 — 'the stars fought against Sisera'). Joshua's prostration and 'what does my lord say to his servant?' (Joshua 5:14) is the response appropriate to a superior military commander — Joshua understood himself as subordinate to this commander. The encounter reframes the entire conquest: Israel's army is not fighting Canaan's cities; YHWH's heavenly army is fighting, and Israel's army is the subordinate partner. The conquest belongs to the divine commander, not to Joshua.
15: "Remove your sandals": 'Remove your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy' (Joshua 5:15) — the command repeats exactly what was said to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:5). This verbal echo identifies the commander's presence with the divine presence at the burning bush: the same holy ground, the same sandal-removal command, the same encounter between a human leader and YHWH's commissioning presence before a great act of deliverance. The parallel is deliberate: Joshua's encounter with the divine commander at Gilgal (just before Jericho) mirrors Moses's encounter at Horeb (just before the Exodus). Both men receive their commissions on holy ground, by the same holy presence, for parallel acts of divine deliverance.
